speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three. We were talking about being in the lager and working. Where was the first lager that you were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I now remember. I was in a few lagers. The last lager, I am very interested to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last lager-- this is what I wanted to say about the Russians. The Russian people, in general, is a good people, a people that suffered for hundreds of hundreds of years, under the Tsar, under communism, under all kinds of cranks. They never got freedom to taste democracy. They don't even know what this is. They're afraid of democracy. They don't know our way of life. Not everybody's a Pushkin or a Dostoyevsky. Not everybody's a Gorky. These are working class Muzhiks. I love them. I like them, the Russian people. And why I like them, I give you a small story. I was in Stalingrad. You know the war was around Stalingrad between the Germans and the Russians. Before the Germans came to Stalingrad, I was in Stalingrad on the way to the [? middle ?], of the way to Samarkand. I was stopped there. In the [INAUDIBLE] family, an old Russian family. They got a wooden house. Oh, very warm there, inside the winter. He lost one hand, and an arm, he lost, in the First World War. When the Germans attacked Russia, he was a communist. He was a member of the Communist Party. So he got food in home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He started to cry, for the Germans started to go and go and go. And these are the words. I would like it if this goes into the archives. [NON-ENGLISH] communism [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Do it was [? buried ?] [INAUDIBLE] is buried [INAUDIBLE] and the Germans, [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is the Russians. He shared with me every piece of bread what he got. This is the real Russian people. And he begged me to run from there. For he said, he believed that the Germans will come soon, here. And he feel there started to be anti-Semitism between the Russian people. Because the Jews speak the same language like the Germans. The-- the-- in general, the Russian, the Muzhik is not educated to know too much the difference. The Jewish language and the German, it looked the same, like they were stepbrothers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he shared with me every piece of bread, the family. And I run away. And I run to-- what I said, to Samarkand. Under the-- being in Samarkand, arrested, I was sent to a few lagers. There are lot of lagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I was very, very sick. They sent me to a lager-- to a-- to a working lager-- but the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what the [NON-ENGLISH] means? [NON-ENGLISH] means you tell them, the [NON-ENGLISH], like the manager from the lager wanted to take me [INAUDIBLE] found out I'm so sick. Why, he needed sick people to die there-- to die there? He needed people to work, healthy people. So he didn't want to take me in. So I left in the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what I got, I was very sick. So they-- nobody wanted to take me in. So they decided to send me back to Tashkent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Tashkent was a lager. They called this [NON-ENGLISH]. This meant a hospital-- a city-- a small city, a town, [NON-ENGLISH] a small town hospital. The whole town is hospitals. They sent me there. When I came there, I didn't believe my own eyes as in Russia [INAUDIBLE] lager. You name it, you got food, what kind of food. And what kind of people is there, you were surprised. One man was there when I land. And they-- they washed me. They clothed me. And I got x-rays and the doctor's attention. I got a bed, my own bed. You know, I lived alone in a room with six people. All nurses are there. And doctors are there. And I got-- the first, they operated on me, on a hemorrhoid there. They haven't got the anaesthetic [INAUDIBLE]. I had very big pains and trouble. Or, they make it, the job. And they saved my life. I got so much with blood, I lost it. And they saved me. I was in that hospital with one named Tsereteli. Mark it down, this. And he was the Secretary of State for the former government, Kerensky government. When the revolution was in Russia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a democratic government came to power. Kerensky was the President. After the Bolsheviks, they were a minority. Make the revolution, and they grabbed the power. And they were arrested. And the secretary-- Kerensky ran away to America. And Tsereteli was the Secretary of State. Stalin arrested him. Stalin and Tsereteli were two friends. In the same school, they went. And they arrested him. They gave him life imprisonment. When I came to the lager, what I said, that is a lager like a paradise of the world. He was there, Tsereteli. And I was in that lager. Every day I got white bread, a piece of butter, a supper, something with meat, rice. You know, I started to feel better. I started to say, I'm a tailor. They needed it there at the hospital to fix the linens, to fix the pajamas. Everybody were in their pajama.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked there in the tailor. Not too many hours-- however many hours I wanted. They're not pressing me.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, a delegation from America, they came. They showed the Americans how the lagers [INAUDIBLE] in Russia. And this was a showcase. If they showed the other lagers, it was a big difference. And here, we really got a good time. And from that lager, I was freed. After-- in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it. I got 10 years. But really, I was there for five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you suddenly freed in 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Polish government. I was a Polish citizen. The Polish government got a deal with the Russian, all prisoners that were from Polish citizenship to be freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came in. I still remember the-- the-- the telegram that came in in Russia. They said, [NON-ENGLISH]. This means that they take it off my-- my-- my-- my case. And that they'd set me free. This are the words that I will remember all my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same day-- it was in a Saturday. The office is closed and everything. Now, since there was there a [NON-ENGLISH], a very good one, that said to me, go. Go, today. And he signed all the papers. Everybody have [INAUDIBLE]. Like, the hospital had to sign. And they was giving out the clothing had to sign. Even the [NON-ENGLISH] from lager signed, it's good for all. If they all got sign it, it's enough. Him-- him sign what [INAUDIBLE]. Since he signed it, he's responsible for everybody. And I went out. I was very interesting. And I went out free. I walked to the station. And I got a paper legal to go to Moscow. They didn't let into Moscow. Nobody could go into Moscow. I got the paper. I wanted to go to the train. A million people were there. Nobody can go into the train. So a policeman took me to the train. And when a policeman from the police coming there and pushed away everybody, don't fight with police. So he pushed everybody away. And he went in with me in the train. He you told somebody to go off in the-- from the shelves. And he gave me the shelf. And I lay down. And it took my two and a half weeks. Actually, it takes 48 hours, maybe, to go to Moscow. It took two and a half week. The train went to Moscow. And in the train, they get you some food. You go in car for hot water. Nobody will take away your shelf where you there. This is-- was going around the train-- the supervisor-- will-- will protect you from this one. So I was not afraid. And to steal-- they have got nothing to steal. I got nothing. [INAUDIBLE] When I went out from the lager, they gave me 10 dry herring. They give me like 5 pound dry bread. They called it [NON-ENGLISH], like the dry bread. What is dried, you call this [INAUDIBLE], four weeks, five weeks, you know. This is the dried in the sun, you know, dark bread. So you can hold it for the whole on the way-- on the way. And they gave me a few ruble. And I got my own. I got when I went out from the lager. I got my own, a few [INAUDIBLE] ruble [INAUDIBLE] and a shirt and underpants, you know, when I went out from the lager. When I came to Moscow, I found out where the Polish embassy is. I went there. I was with the papers. I showed them where I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Polish government in the embassy, they called me. And an officer came in. They called me in in a room. And they told me to take off everything what I wear and to leave there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was real frightened a little. But he was like an officer. You can see his uniform. Now, he was an invalid with one hand. I took out everything. I took off. I took out the money I had. And I took a bath. They brought me in-- new, brand new. Like a-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], like a winter jacket, winter pants, and inside pants, socks and a pair of new shoes and a-- a hat. And they called me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time in my life-- and I'm saying it's first time in my life-- I eat in the embassy, rice mixed with meat, silver plate, and white-- white bread, like Italian bread, some assortment, and a glass of wine. And I didn't know is this a dream, or the truth? And it was true. They were treating you like a hero.

speaker: interviewee
text: They find out in the paper that I was [? an ?] [? old ?] communist. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in the embassy for four weeks. I got food and money. I went to the Bolshoi theater a few times. And I-- every day, I went into the embassy to beg them. I wanted to go home. I wanted to find out-- my sisters, my mother. I didn't know the [? home ?]. And I-- after four weeks, I went home. It's not the point. [INAUDIBLE] I came to Lodz. And I went to the apartment where we lived, I found out the real harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, Auschwitz? I got nobody. Most of the people there is killed. I found one guy that used to live in the same building. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. He told me the story about my sister Laya.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called her singing Laya. Because she always-- in the ghetto, she went around and sang and begged for a piece of bread. She got a very nice voice. So everybody gave her a piece of bread in the ghetto. And he told me in the train what they said, that from the ghetto, Lodz ghetto, to Auschwitz, she was there. She was killed on the spot there. She started to sing when she came off the train. She forgot, though, she was a little-- you know, after so many beating up. They killed her on the spot, there. He told me. And as a matter of fact, she was killed by them, by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So being in Lodz, I know I have a brother in Canada. I had nobody. What to do? So I sent a letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember it from memory, my brother used to live in Toronto on a Uchlid street. And Uchlid is Euclid, actually. And we didn't know the spelling-- the-- the-- the pronunciation-- how to pronounce. We didn't know this is the U-C-H-L-I-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we called it, we said, Uchlid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, it's Euclid, 11 and 1/2 Euclid Avenue. I send the letter. The spelling and the writing was correct. The letter came. Now, between this time and the war time, my brother's son was the highest student in technology in Toronto. And they sent him to the MIT-- MTI? MIT.

speaker: interviewee
text: MIT. Yes, MIT. In MIT, they found out that he is a genius in physics. And my brother got only one son with a daughter. They didn't let him go alone. So, since to America, to go, you have to go by quarter. My brother got the permission to move to America right away, because of his son. My nephew was a student [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Professor [INAUDIBLE] for a time. And he got high marks. As a matter of fact, now, my nephew is Dr. Emanuel Rotenberg Doctor Professor of nuclear science. The whole family from the Rotenbergs, even from the mother's side, even from my mother's side-- because my mother and father, they're married. They were cousins was-- got a technical inside, like a gene. And I told you my mother's brother was the inventor for the carburetor, why the German hold him. My father was the first one to make it. I don't know how they call this in Canada. You know how Canada got, in the old country, 50 years ago, [INAUDIBLE] machine they called this. I don't know-- he spoke the German [INAUDIBLE]. Put in gasoline, you pump it. [INAUDIBLE] gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, like a stove.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- like a wooden stove, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father took this apart. And he make the lamp, a gas lamp. The whole city come to see the-- the-- the wonder. The-- the-- my father was really-- we all in the family. I'm not going to take credit for something. [INAUDIBLE] electrical things, my family know. And they called me, my sister, something-- the telephone is broke, or an iron is broke, or a television even, or a telephone. They called me, and I fix everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Rotenberg, you were starting to tell me that you wrote-- you were in Lodz. And you wrote a letter to your brother on Euclid street in Toronto. But from what you just said, it seems your brother wasn't living there anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was not living. No, postman know the letter come from Europe. After the war arrived, the people, they were shocked when they hear what's going on. The Uncle Nathan- what you see there-- Nathan Rotenberg lived the-- the next house. So the postman gave it, the letter, not to my brother-- but my brother moved between this time, to the States. Excuse me. They give it to my uncle. My uncle [INAUDIBLE] he sent the letter to my brother. He read the letter. I'm alive. And [INAUDIBLE] I'm alive, they find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother know-- he got a friend. [INAUDIBLE] his friend's brother was in Lodz. And he was established in Lodz. He got a factory of clothing right after the war. So they called him. A [? so-and-so ?] person will come give you money. How much you wanted? And my brother-- when I sent the letter-- in the sender's name, I made [? poster and stamp. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to the mailbox, to the post office, to-- to find out if some mail is there, they give me the letter from my brother what this [? poster and ?] [? stamp. ?] Reading the letter, there is an address and a name, where to go to get money. And in the letter, he mentioned that my sister Bluma, what I said here, is alive. And everybody-- nobody's alive. Only Bluma is safe. And she lived in Germany, in Braunschweig. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I took a few dollars there. Not much, because I'm afraid. Because every dollar is [INAUDIBLE]. If I take hundreds of dollars, [INAUDIBLE]. You know, [INAUDIBLE] cost a bread. Or, I was afraid, so I take a few dollars. I don't remember how much. Not too much. And I was in Poland. And I tried-- in every way, I tried to go over to see my sisters. Now, since I was in the Communist party and a lot of communists recognized me, they wanted to take me in. And they give me a uniform. [INAUDIBLE] I said, oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes, with one eye. With the other eye, I look where is an opening to run away. Because being in Russia, and seeing in Russia, what communism is-- uh, uh, uh. It's not my taste. It's not my cup of tea. So I with them for a while. In [INAUDIBLE] time, I went to the border city. Klodzko, they called it. And I went over the border to Czechoslovakia, in Austria, from Austria to Germany to see my sister. And I run away from these communists and the Polish communists. I didn't want to have nothing. I realized it's false. For Jews, it's communism-- poison.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you found your sister and in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You found your sister in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found my sister in-- in-- in Germany. And she got a girlfriend. And the girlfriend is my wife now. She was the matchmaker. I didn't give her, you know, not one dollar for marrying me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She wanted some [INAUDIBLE] money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me, the first time you-- you met your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife. Well. The first time when I came to seeing my wife, she was very beautiful. If I show you a picture, you'd be surprised. Not the picture, right, four weeks after the lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I-- I-- my brother made out papers to Canada. I came to Canada. When I came, I was engaged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a short time, she came here with the domestic group, domestic there.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of girls, they bring domestic here. You know, the Jewish Congress looked for every way, how to bring Jewish kids. Under domestic, under tailors, under furriers, call it what you want it, just bring it here. I give them credit for this one. She came under the domestic. Oh, the auntie what is now 98-year-old, she sent a letter to the government to release from the domestic [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] application. She is bound under the government. But they brought her here. The government paid for her to bring the domestic. We will pay the cost to release, because she find here who's her boyfriend-- her-- boyfriend. But she wanted to marry. And she wanted to marry [INAUDIBLE]. So they released her from the obligation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I married the same day as my sister. We got a double wedding. And so started our life, me and my wife with 190. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, you know, with inflation time, you not get 120. 190 minimum. Sweet and sour. When you were first married and living here in Toronto with your wife, what kind of work did you do to support yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was tailoring with a tailor. My wife, for a short time, for a few months only, she was working here, you know. And after I tried, this one, this one, I'm not a born businessman. I'm saying like-- like it's-- you know. My brother was a born businessman. He was. [INAUDIBLE] died. I have to tell you a story of my father. When he was alive, he said before, I will never be a businessman. And my brother will be a businessman. And I tell you a story. You'll want to hear this one. My father, one time, took him to Lodz and give it to me. He said, Peretz, take it to [? Shlomo ?]. Give to [? Shlomo ?] to him the dollar. I got a loan from him, so I give it back to him, the dollar. I took to him the dollar, the packet. And I'm going to go to [? Shlomo ?] to give him what my father sent. He said, wait. He called my brother and gave him $100. Now, taking the dollars, I bought a [INAUDIBLE]. And gave it to [INAUDIBLE] back. When my brother got to him the dollar, he opened it up and counted to him the dollar. Me, he gave it, I put it in the pocket. He said, he will be a businessman, and you not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? You see it. He's a businessman. When I give to him the dollar, never mind [INAUDIBLE]. He counted the hundred dollars.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a businessman. And when I give to him the dollar, you put it right away in the bag-- in the pocket. You never will be a good businessman. He was right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were a tailor, at first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was a tailor, a short time. And I didn't like it, you know. I realized, being a tailor, I'll never go too far. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- you were working for somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I worked for a short time. And after, I started to make it a clothing store that didn't working. I bought a cigar store, like a variety store. We were both working very hard at the variety store. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't make it so much money. We make it money, not because we earned it. We make it money, because we didn't spend it. Never got time, haven't got the place. We haven't got no way to spend. When I buy the store, the variety store, I got a car. And the moment I buy the variety store, the car was in the garage. I never use it. I haven't got time to use it. What I buy-- a-- a salesman's coming in. They bring it in everything. So I sold the car. Now, after a while, my children started to complain. We're working, you know, only once in a year, Yom Kippur, [INAUDIBLE] I got close the store. The children start to grow up and to complain we never eat together like a family. And I got a few dollars saved. So we decided to go in another business, a clothing business. Since there is when [INAUDIBLE] Italian people, I decided to go in-- in clothing for children, for Italian-- for mostly, Italian kids. And I got that store done for and paid. But mostly Italians that lived in that section. And I started to cater for them more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though your father said that you wouldn't be such a good businessmen, here you were. You-- you were really running your own business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got my own business. And then [INAUDIBLE] day, the Italians moved out from that section. And they moved up north. And my store was catered mostly, 99%, to Italians. And I was stuck with the whole store, with the whole everything. Then, moved in, Greek people, Macedonians. And they're not [INAUDIBLE] with everything. I have to give credit some to the Italians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it-- the Italian children's store didn't work out the way you hoped that it would.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where a business would got the smell before. And I got the smell after.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went broke.
